message.content: I am concerned about the shape WSG has taken.  BSG I think is clearer: clean specification of patterns.  WSG seems to have a lot of circular commentary rather than simple 'this is good' 'this is bad' statements.  Assess.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Assess WSG vs BSG specification clarity

content.content:
1	# WSG — Windows Scripting Guide
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	WSG codifies the discipline for embedding remote shell actions (PowerShell or
6	bash) inside ssh sessions to Windows hosts, with full error trapping at every
7	layer. WSG extends BCG's bash discipline into the ssh-to-Windows transport
8	stack — where curia bash code authors a string that traverses ssh → Windows
9	OpenSSH → cmd.exe → child shell → script execution.
10	
11	WSG is empirically derived. Every established rule has a verification probe
12	that demonstrates the failure mode the rule prevents. Open questions are
13	flagged explicitly and resolved by separate experiment paces, not by
14	conjecture.
15	
16	## Core Philosophy
17	
18	**Every layer is a fault domain.** A character that means one thing in curia
19	bash means another in cmd.exe, another in the Windows argv parser, another
20	in PowerShell's `-Command` parser, and another in Linux bash. The transport
21	**transforms** the body at each boundary. Errors at any layer can be
22	silently swallowed if not explicitly trapped.
23	
24	**The cmd.exe layer is invisible from curia.** The user's command is wrapped
25	in `cmd.exe /c <command>` before any other shell sees it. Cmd.exe's quoting
26	rules — `"..."` only; no single-quote support; idiosyncratic `\` handling —
27	bite even bodies that look bash-correct from the curia.
28	
29	**Some object output formatters are lazy.** PowerShell flushes string
30	output (Write-Host, Write-Output of strings) eagerly; some cmdlets'
31	formatter cycles flush only on full pipeline completion, so calling
32	`exit` mid-body (or trailer-side) discards their unformatted output.
33	The set of lazy cmdlets is not universal — `Get-LocalUser` is proven
34	lazy, `Get-Item` is proven eager. Treat the lazy default as the safe
35	assumption (PS-2). String emission always survives.
36	
37	**Trap, don't trust.** Each rule below assumes that any error which can
38	plausibly be silenced WILL be silenced if not explicitly trapped. The bias
39	is toward defensive instrumentation: surface every non-zero exit, every
40	empty stdout, every unflushed buffer, with a forensic temp file naming the
41	boundary that swallowed it.
42	
43	**Rules enumerate; they don't illustrate.** Each rule below names a closed
44	set of allowed shapes. Anything not enumerated is forbidden, even if it
45	*looks* morally similar to an allowed form. Do not reason "this is *like*
46	one expression because it's *like* one effect" — that path admits a fourth
47	shape the rule never sanctioned, and the rationalization propagates to
48	every call site that copies the precedent. When in doubt, decompose into
49	more round-trips: the cost of an extra ssh hop is small; the cost of a
50	loophole is structural.
51	
52	## Transport Stack
53	
54	A typical chain for `zbujb_admin_powershell` or `zbujb_admin_exec`:
55	
56	```
57	1. Local bash (curia)            builds ssh argument string
58	2. ssh client                    forwards command to remote
59	3. Windows OpenSSH sshd          spawns DefaultShell (cmd.exe)
60	4. cmd.exe /c <command>          parses tokens, respects "...", spawns child
61	5. Child shell (one of):
62	   a. powershell.exe -Command "<body>"     runs body in PowerShell
63	   b. wsl.exe ... bash -c "<body>"          forwards to Linux bash in WSL
64	   c. C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c "..." runs Cygwin bash
65	6. Body subprocess(es)           child processes inherit parent's FD 0
66	```
67	
68	Each step has its own quoting model. Each can fail silently.
69	
70	## Established Rules
71	
72	### ❌ PS-1: Trailing `exit $LASTEXITCODE` without LASTEXITCODE init
73	
74	In a fresh `powershell -Command` invocation with no native command run,
75	`$LASTEXITCODE` is `$null`. PowerShell's typed comparison treats `$null -ne 0`
76	as `True`. A trailer like `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }`
77	always fires for cmdlet-only bodies, calling `exit $null` (which exits 0 in
78	practice). The `exit` short-circuits PowerShell's lazy object-formatter
79	pipeline, discarding any cmdlet output buffered by Out-Default that hasn't
80	flushed to stdout yet.
81	
82	```powershell
83	# ❌ Eats Get-LocalUser's table output (returns CRLF only)
84	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
85	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
86	
87	# ✅ Initialize so the branch only fires on real native command failures
88	powershell -Command "$LASTEXITCODE = 0;
89	                     Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
90	                     if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit $LASTEXITCODE }"
91	```
92	
93	Probe pair (verification):
94	
95	```
96	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
97	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
98	# stdout → CRLF only
99	
100	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> \
101	  "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$LASTEXITCODE = 0; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
102	# stdout → full LocalUser table
103	```
104	
105	### ❌ PS-2: `exit` mid-body before object formatters flush
106	
107	PowerShell flushes string output eagerly but *some* cmdlet object outputs
108	lazily (Out-Default → Format-Table → render). `exit` aborts the formatter
109	pipeline. Lazy outputs that haven't flushed are discarded. The cmdlets
110	that exhibit this lazy behavior are formatter-cycle-specific: `Get-LocalUser`
111	from `Microsoft.PowerShell.LocalAccounts` is proven lazy (empty stdout
112	when followed by `exit 0`); `Get-Item` from the FileSystem provider is
113	proven eager (table survives `exit 0`). Treat the rule as the safe
114	default: assume any cmdlet output is lazy unless you've checked. See
115	the OQ-6 section of `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
116	for the empirical baseline.
117	
118	```powershell
119	# ❌ Get-LocalUser's table is lost; Get-Date's string survives
120	powershell -Command "Get-Date; Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo'; exit 0"
121	
122	# ✅ Materialize objects to strings before any potential exit
123	powershell -Command "Get-LocalUser -Name 'foo' | Out-String; ..."
124	```
125	
126	If a wrapper provides `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` initialization (PS-1), this rule
127	is moot for happy-path bodies. Failure paths still need string-materialization
128	guards if any cmdlet output must survive the trailer's exit branch.
129	
130	### ❌ SH-1: Script via stdin to bash (heredoc form / `bash -s`)
131	
132	When ssh feeds a script to remote bash via stdin — `bash -s` on the remote
133	with heredoc on the local side, or `echo BODY | bash` chains on the remote —
134	bash reads commands from FD 0. Child processes spawned by that bash inherit
135	FD 0, connected to the script-providing pipe. A child that reads FD 0
136	(notably Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop) consumes script bytes
137	bash hadn't yet parsed. Bash hits EOF prematurely, exits 0 without errors,
138	and remaining commands silently never execute.
139	
140	This is BCG's stdin-consumption discipline (BCG line 1388) at a different
141	scope.
142	
143	```bash
144	# ❌ Script via heredoc/stdin; net.exe (or any FD-0 reader) eats bytes
145	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -s" <<SCRIPT
146	set -euo pipefail
147	net.exe user 'foo' /add ... > /dev/null
148	useradd 'foo'                       # may silently never run
149	SCRIPT
150	
151	# ✅ Body as bash -c argument; bash reads body from -c, not FD 0
152	# (one statement per ssh round-trip, per SH-10; the multi-statement
153	#  shape this fix originally used was retired by pace ₢A-AA9)
154	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"net.exe user 'foo' /add ...\""
155	```
156	
157	Verification: cycle-1 vs cycle-3 of correct-wsl-user-model (`₢A-AAv`).
158	Heredoc form silently dropped useradd; args form propagated correctly,
159	proven by `getent passwd bujuw_user` returning the entry post-step-3.
160	
161	### ❌ SH-2: Newlines in bodies through cmd.exe
162	
163	Cmd.exe is line-oriented. Newlines in `"..."` quoted args are unreliable
164	across the cmd.exe → child-binary boundary. Bodies must be single-line.
165	
166	Under SH-10 a body is a single statement anyway, so the line-orientation
167	constraint is rarely the binding one — but it still applies to the
168	characters inside that one statement (e.g. no newlines inside a long
169	pipeline written for readability).
170	
171	```bash
172	# ❌ Newlines in body; cmd.exe may fragment
173	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"
174	useradd foo
175	\""
176	
177	# ✅ Single-line, single-statement body
178	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"useradd foo\""
179	```
180	
181	`;`-joining multiple statements onto one line is **forbidden separately**
182	by SH-10 (single-statement contract). SH-2 is purely about newlines.
183	
184	### ✅ SH-3: Outer `"..."` with `\"` for embedded double-quotes
185	
186	The Windows argv parser (used by wsl.exe and most Windows binaries) honors
187	`\"` inside `"..."` as an escape for literal `"`. This survives cmd.exe and
188	reaches Linux bash as `"`. Cmd.exe itself does NOT process `\"`; it passes
189	the bytes to the child whose argv parser handles the escape.
190	
191	```bash
192	# ✅ Embedded " in body
193	ssh ... "wsl.exe ... bash -c \"echo SAW: \\\"hello\\\"\""
194	```
195	
196	Probe: `bash -c "echo SAW: \"hello\""` → stdout `SAW: hello` (quotes
197	stripped by bash's own parsing of the unescaped `"`).
198	
199	(The previous illustrative `trap 'rm -f ...' EXIT` example was retired
200	in pace ₢A-AA9: `trap` is a state-machine construct, forbidden in
201	single-statement bodies per SH-10.)
202	
203	### ✅ PS-3: PowerShell single-quoted strings survive nesting
204	
205	Inside the outer `"..."` of `powershell -Command "..."`, single-quoted
206	strings (`'Stop'`, `'username'`) pass through cleanly. Cmd.exe does not
207	honor single quotes for arg-grouping but treats them as literal characters;
208	PowerShell parses its body and recognizes `'...'` as PS string literals.
209	
210	```bash
211	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
212	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'bujuw_user'\""
213	```
214	
215	This is why `zbujb_admin_powershell` consistently uses single quotes for PS
216	string literals — they nest reliably through every layer.
217	
218	### ✅ PS-4: PS-2's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
219	
220	The PS-2 lazy-flush is a property of the PowerShell body, not of how
221	PowerShell was launched. The same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits
222	empty stdout via direct cmd.exe→powershell, cygwin→powershell.exe, and
223	wsl.exe→bash→powershell.exe. The fix (Out-String materialization, or
224	PS-1's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0` so the trailer doesn't fire) is also
225	transport-agnostic. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
226	§OQ-6.
227	
228	### ❌ SH-4: cmd.exe does NOT process `$()` or `$var`
229	
230	Verified by direct probe: `cmd.exe /c echo "SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` emits the
231	literal string `"SHELL=$0 X=$(uname)"` with no expansion. Cmd.exe's
232	variable syntax is `%var%` and it has no command substitution. Therefore
233	the outer cmd.exe layer is NOT the layer that processes shell variable
234	expansion or command substitution in transit.
235	
236	This rule is structural; the corollary is OQ-1 (something else does eat `$`
237	in some chains).
238	
239	### ❌ SH-5: BCG bans extend across the transport boundary
240	
241	The bash code we author at the curia AND the bash code we author for remote
242	execution are both subject to BCG. In particular:
243	
244	- **No heredocs anywhere.** The transport must not use `bash -s` with
245	  heredoc input (SH-1 covers the failure mode); module callers must not
246	  build remote bodies via heredoc.
247	- **No pipelines inside `$()`.** This applies to module code (e.g. the
248	  ill-fated `z_b64=$(printf ... | base64 | tr -d ...)` transport) and to
249	  bodies authored for remote execution.
250	- **No unguarded `$()`.** Remote bodies that use command substitution must
251	  guard against silent failure the same way curia code does.
252	
253	Top-level pipelines (not inside `$()`) are permitted in remote bodies as
254	shape 2 of SH-10 (a pipeline is one operation, optionally preceded by
255	`set -o pipefail`). The historical `$(mktemp)` allowance for remote bodies
256	was retired by pace ₢A-AA9 along with the multi-statement contract: any
257	pattern that needed a remote temp file (e.g. the original plant-key) was a
258	multi-statement state machine and is now forbidden by SH-10. Bash on the
259	curia owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect.
260	
261	### ❌ SH-6: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv
262	
263	The wsl.exe argv-to-Linux-invocation processor performs `$name` and
264	`${name}` substitution against its own (Linux-side, root-shell startup)
265	environment before bash receives the body. Names present in that
266	environment substitute to their values; undefined names substitute to
267	empty. **Cygwin bash does not exhibit this behavior. cmd.exe alone (per
268	SH-4) also does not.** `$(...)` command substitution is unaffected (the
269	`(` after `$` does not match the `$name`/`${name}` token shape).
270	
271	Per-letter escape table:
272	
273	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
274	|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
275	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
276	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
277	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |
278	
279	Probe pair (verification, w-letter — invoked through `bujb_privileged_ssh`,
280	which is a pass-through wrapper not bound by SH-10's single-statement
281	contract; the multi-statement body is essential to demonstrate the
282	substitution behavior):
283	
284	```
285	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
286	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
287	# stdout: (empty)
288	
289	# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
290	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
291	# stdout: HELLO
292	```
293	
294	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-1, §OQ-2,
295	§OQ-4.
296	
297	### ✅ SH-7: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports
298	
299	Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through every tested transport:
300	cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe → bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and native
301	Windows binaries called via wsl.exe interop. The single statement in the
302	body's shape (per SH-10) is also the body's exit code — it reaches the
303	curia unchanged via the wrapper's ssh exit status, and the caller absorbs
304	it via `|| buc_die "..."`. No `set -e` discipline is needed inside the
305	body, because there is nothing after the single statement that `set -e`
306	would protect.
307	
308	`set -o pipefail` IS needed for shape 2 (pipeline) when the pipeline's
309	intent is to fail if any stage fails — pipefail is a precondition of
310	the pipeline's exit-code semantics, not a separate operation, and is
311	permitted inside the same statement (see SH-10 exception).
312	
313	Probe (w-letter, false propagation):
314	
315	```
316	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> 'wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "false"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
317	# EXIT=1
318	```
319	
320	See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` §OQ-5.
321	
322	### ✅ SH-8: Multi-line bodies via file feed (when SH-2 doesn't fit)
323	
324	When a body materially exceeds what `;`-join (SH-2) can express, the
325	canonical pattern is two-phase: phase 1 ships the body as a remote file
326	via PowerShell `Set-Content` or `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText`; phase 2
327	runs `wsl.exe ... bash /path/to/file` (or `cygwin bash --login -c "bash
328	/path/to/file"`). Side benefit: bypasses wsl.exe's argv `$`-substitution
329	(SH-6) since the script body comes from disk, not argv.
330	
331	Caveats:
332	- PS `Set-Content` writes CRLF. Either use an LF-explicit writer:
333	
334	  ```powershell
335	  [System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, ($lines -join "`n"))
336	  ```
337	
338	  or normalize CRLF→LF into a *second* file before exec.
339	- Do NOT pipe the file into `bash` via stdin; that reintroduces SH-1's
340	  stdin-consumption hazard. Use `bash /path/to/file`, not `bash <file`.
341	- The two-phase approach is not atomic; the caller flow must clean up
342	  the remote temp file.
343	
344	Default discipline remains SH-2; SH-8 applies only when bodies don't fit
345	one line. See `Memos/memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md`
346	§OQ-7.
347	
348	### ❌ SH-9: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport
349	
350	When a command line is routed `bash (curia) → ssh → Windows OpenSSH →
351	cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with no PowerShell or remote bash
352	layer to interpret quotes, single quotes around args are passed
353	through to the binary as part of its argv. This is the negative form
354	of PS-3.
355	
356	```bash
357	# ❌ Single quotes survive cmd.exe and become part of wsl.exe's argv.
358	# wsl.exe sees the path as `'C:\path'` (literal quotes) and fails.
359	ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs' '...' --version 2"
360	
361	# ✅ Double quotes via `\"...\"`. After bash escape they reach the wire
362	# as `"..."`; cmd.exe strips them per its native argv parser; wsl.exe
363	# receives clean paths.
364	ssh "$WORKLOAD@$HOST" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\rbtww-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
365	```
366	
367	The asymmetry: PS-3 (single quotes survive nesting) applies when there
368	is a PowerShell layer that recognizes `'...'` as a string-literal
369	delimiter. SH-9 applies when there is no such layer — only cmd.exe
370	between the wire and the binary. Single quotes are not a
371	transport-universal "safe" choice; they require a layer that
372	interprets them.
373	
374	Decision rule: pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter** in
375	the transport stack:
376	
377	| Innermost interpreter | Quote form for embedded args |
378	|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
379	| PowerShell            | `'...'` (PS string literal)   |
380	| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)  |
381	| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips) |
382	
383	Empirical site: `zbujb_garrison_w_init_wsl` in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
384	— the `wsl --import` invocation in garrison-w's three-namespace
385	redesign (BUSJGW).
386	
387	### ❌ SH-10: Bash bodies are single statements
388	
389	A `zbujb_admin_exec` body MUST be exactly one of these three shapes — no
390	others:
391	
392	1. One simple command (with arguments, options, and redirections).
393	2. One pipeline (`cmd1 | cmd2 | ...`), optionally preceded by
394	   `set -o pipefail` to make the pipeline's exit status surface the
395	   first non-zero stage.
396	3. One simple command whose argument is itself an inner-shell body
397	   (e.g. `wsl.exe --user root install ...`, `sudo -n install ...`).
398	
399	The enumeration is constructive (Core Philosophy, *Rules enumerate; they
400	don't illustrate*). No other bash construct may appear at the body's
401	top level. Forbidden constructs include `if`/`then`/`else`/`fi`,
402	`for`/`while`/`until`, `case`, `&&`/`||` chains, `;`-separated
403	statements, intermediate `var=...` assignments, `trap`, function
404	definitions, and `(...)` subshells used to scope state. A single
405	`if`-guarded effect like
406	`if test -e '...'; then sudo -n rm '...'; fi` is also forbidden — even
407	though it has only one "real" effect, the body contains a decision
408	(the test) and an action (the consequent). Decisions belong in bash on
409	the curia. See "Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below for the
410	canonical procedure.
411	
412	If a body needs N intermediate values or N decisions, run N round-trips:
413	capture state via `zbujb_admin_exec` (its stdout flows through `$(...)`
414	on the curia), decide in bash, dispatch unconditional effects via
415	`zbujb_admin_exec`. Bash on the curia owns the state machine; the
416	remote-side body is one effect.
417	
418	Exception: `set -o pipefail` is permitted INSIDE the same statement as
419	a pipeline (shape 2), since pipefail is a precondition of the
420	pipeline's exit-code semantics, not a separate operation.
421	
422	Empirical baseline: every `zbujb_admin_exec` call site in
423	`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` follows the single-statement shape after pace
424	₢A-AA9. The historical violator was `zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_key`,
425	which used a six-statement state machine
426	(`set -euo pipefail; ztmp=$(mktemp); trap 'rm -f "$ztmp"' EXIT;
427	echo b64 | openssl > "$ztmp"; install ... "$ztmp" target`) with
428	cross-statement `$ztmp` and a cleanup trap. The repair eliminated the
429	state machine entirely by piping the workload key from the curia over
430	ssh stdin into `install /dev/stdin`, leaving one simple command.
431	
432	```bash
433	# ❌ Compound state machine — six statements, cross-statement variable, trap
434	zbujb_admin_exec b "set -euo pipefail" \
435	  "ztmp=\$(mktemp)" \
436	  "trap 'rm -f \"\${ztmp}\"' EXIT" \
437	  "echo '<b64>' | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > \"\${ztmp}\"" \
438	  "sudo -n install -m 600 -o user -g user \"\${ztmp}\" '<target>'"
439	
440	# ❌ Single `if`-guarded effect — body decides AND acts
441	zbujb_admin_exec b "if test -e '${z_path}'; then sudo -n rm '${z_path}'; fi"
442	
443	# ✅ Single simple command; key flows from curia file via ssh stdin into /dev/stdin
444	zbujb_admin_exec b "sudo -n install -m 600 -o '${z_wlu}' -g '${z_wlu}' /dev/stdin '${z_target}'" \
445	  < "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"
446	
447	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
448	local z_exit=0
449	zbujb_admin_exec b "stat '${z_path}' > /dev/null 2>&1" || z_exit=$?
450	if [[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]; then
451	  zbujb_admin_exec b "sudo -n rm '${z_path}'" || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
452	fi
453	```
454	
455	The `< "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"` redirection on the curia attaches
456	the file to bash's FD 0, which the wrapper's ssh inherits, which
457	sshd forwards to the remote command's FD 0, which the remote bash
458	exposes to its single-statement body as `/dev/stdin`. The body itself
459	remains shape 1 (one simple command with options + path argument);
460	the file content never appears in argv. SH-1's stdin-consumption
461	hazard is structurally avoided because the body is a single command
462	that explicitly reads `/dev/stdin` — no second command exists to be
463	silently starved by leftover bytes.
464	
465	### ❌ PS-5: PowerShell bodies are single expressions
466	
467	A `zbujb_admin_powershell` (or `zbujb_powershell_capture`) body MUST be
468	exactly one of these three shapes — no others:
469	
470	1. One cmdlet call (with arguments, parameters, and pipelined formatters
471	   like `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
472	2. One native binary call (with arguments).
473	3. One native binary with an inner-shell body (e.g.
474	   `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).
475	
476	The enumeration is constructive (Core Philosophy, *Rules enumerate; they
477	don't illustrate*). No other PowerShell construct may appear at the body's
478	top level. Forbidden constructs include `if`, `try`/`catch`, `foreach`,
479	`while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||` operators, `;`-separated statements,
480	intermediate `$var` assignments, and ternary `?:`. A single `if`-guarded
481	effect like `if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden
482	— even though it has only one "real" effect, the body contains a decision
483	(the test) and an action (the consequent). Decisions belong in bash. See
484	"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below for the canonical procedure.
485	
486	If a body needs N intermediate values or N decisions, run N round-trips:
487	capture state via `zbujb_powershell_capture` into bash variables, decide
488	in bash, dispatch unconditional effects via `zbujb_admin_powershell`. Bash
489	owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect.
490	
491	Empirical baseline: every `zbujb_admin_powershell` call site in the module
492	follows single-expression shape — `Get-LocalUser ...`, `Test-Path ...`,
493	`Get-Acl ...`, `icacls ...`, `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`, etc. The original
494	violator (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`, profile registration via
495	`$sid=...; $path=...; New-Item; New-ItemProperty`) produced a quoting
496	failure (reg.exe `Invalid syntax` on the `Windows NT` space) because the
497	compound shape interleaved PS interpolation, native-binary argv handling,
498	and registry-path building in one body. Decomposing into bash-orchestrated
499	single-expression calls eliminated the failure class. A second documented
500	violation cycle in
501	`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` records
502	the `if`-as-guard rationalization that motivates the explicit
503	forbidden-constructs list above.
504	
505	Exception: the wrapper-side prelude
506	(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; ${z_body}; if ...`)
507	is library code written once and exercised by all callers. Caller-side
508	bodies follow the single-expression rule.
509	
510	```bash
511	# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
512	zbujb_admin_powershell "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
513	
514	# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
515	zbujb_admin_powershell "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
516	
517	# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
518	z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") || buc_die "..."
519	z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
520	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
521	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
522	
523	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
524	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
525	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
526	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
527	fi
528	```
529	
530	### ❌ PS-6: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them
531	
532	If a remote command needs a string with embedded variables (registry path,
533	file path, SID), the bash caller builds the string by expanding
534	`${bash_var}` into the body before sending. The PS body receives the
535	already-resolved string as a literal (PS single-quoted, or
536	argument-bound).
537	
538	Rationale: bash escape rules cascade once. The bash → ssh → cmd.exe →
539	PS-literal-string chain is well-understood. Adding PS-side interpolation
540	(`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution model on top; subtle bugs
541	(like the PS-7 native-binary space-quoting trap) compound across layers
542	and become harder to diagnose.
543	
544	```bash
545	# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
546	zbujb_admin_powershell "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"
547	
548	# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
549	z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
550	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
551	```
552	
553	### ❌ PS-7: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries
554	
555	PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
556	schtasks.exe) has documented quirks with embedded spaces and quotes.
557	A registry path containing `Windows NT` (one space) breaks `reg.exe`
558	invoked through PS even when the path is held in a PS variable — empirical
559	in this session's diagnostic cycle (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715`,
560	pre-decomposition).
561	
562	Rule: for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive via `New-Item` /
563	`New-ItemProperty`, ACLs via `Get-Acl`, account ops via `Get-LocalUser`),
564	use PS cmdlets — they handle their own paths transparently. For
565	native-binary effects (reg.exe, schtasks.exe), either pass already-resolved
566	bash literals that don't traverse PS interpolation, or drop the PS layer
567	entirely and invoke through cmd.exe directly (OpenSSH-Win32's default
568	shell).
569	
570	```bash
571	# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
572	zbujb_admin_powershell "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"
573	
574	# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
575	zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
576	```
577	
578	### ❌ PS-8: Error suppression is not idempotency
579	
580	When a destructive cmdlet (`Remove-Item`, `Unregister-*`, `Stop-Service`,
581	`Remove-LocalUser`) might fail because its target is absent, the temptation
582	is to reach for an error-suppression flag and call the body "idempotent":
583	
584	- `-ErrorAction Ignore`
585	- `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`
586	- `2>$null` redirection
587	- `try { ... } catch { }` with empty or discarding catch
588	
589	Forbidden on destructive actions. Error-suppression flags swallow **every**
590	error class, not just "target absent." Permission denied, file locked, ACL
591	refusal, path-too-long, provider-not-loaded — all silently absorbed. The
592	cmdlet returns success, `$LASTEXITCODE` stays 0, the wrapper's trailer
593	takes the no-fire branch, and bash sees a clean exit. The destructive
594	action might not have actually happened, and we have no way to know.
595	
596	This is the "silently swallowed" failure class WSG opens with (Core
597	Philosophy, *Trap, don't trust*). Idempotency MUST come from explicit
598	state capture in bash followed by a conditional unconditional action — see
599	"Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern" below.
600	
601	Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**. `New-Item
602	... | Out-Null` discards the cmdlet's *return object* and is a routine
603	hygiene practice (the cmdlet still throws on actual error). `Remove-Item
604	... -ErrorAction Ignore` suppresses *errors*, converting "permission
605	denied" into apparent success. PS-8 forbids the latter on destructive
606	actions.
607	
608	Probe-side use is permitted. `Get-LocalUser -Name '...' -ErrorAction
609	SilentlyContinue` on a non-destructive query, where "user not found" is
610	the answer we want and stdout-empty is how we read it, is exactly correct.
611	The carve-out: error suppression is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is
612	to *return state*, forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.
613	
614	```bash
615	# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
616	zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
617	zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
618	zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"
619	
620	# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
621	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || buc_die "..."
622	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
623	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || buc_die "..."
624	fi
625	
626	# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
627	zbujb_admin_powershell "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
628	```
629	
630	Empirical site: this rule was minted after a session in which `-ErrorAction
631	Ignore` was reached for to escape a PS-5 `if`-guard violation, substituting
632	an OQ-1 silent-swallow for the structural one. See
633	`Memos/memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md`.
634	
635	## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern
636	
637	PS-5's and SH-10's shared closing line — *"Bash owns the state machine;
638	the remote-side body is one effect"* — names the canonical procedure for
639	any decision that would otherwise live in a remote body (PS or bash).
640	Capture-Decide-Dispatch (CDD) is a three-step recipe:
641	
642	1. **Capture** — run a single-expression probe and return its stdout
643	   into a bash variable on the curia.
644	   - PS bodies: `zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE "<single-expression probe>"`
645	     (PS-5-clean: cmdlet call or native binary call, no `if`, no
646	     compounds). The wrapper strips Windows CR
647	     (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:487`); the bash variable holds clean text.
648	   - Bash bodies: `$(zbujb_admin_exec LETTER "<single-statement probe>")`
649	     (SH-10-clean: one simple command with redirections, e.g.
650	     `stat '${z_path}' > /dev/null 2>&1`).
651	   - Absorb capture failure with `|| buc_die "..."` if the probe must
652	     succeed, or `|| z_var=""` (PS) / `|| z_exit=$?` (bash, when probing
653	     by exit status) if a fundus-side absence is a legitimate pre-state.
654	
655	2. **Decide** — bash conditional. For PS booleans, see "PowerShell
656	   boolean serialization" below. For text/list output, use
657	   `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$z_var"` for exact-line membership, or
658	   `case ... in ... esac` / `[[ "$z_var" == ... ]]` for value compare.
659	   For bash exit-status probes, `[[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]`.
660	
661	3. **Dispatch** — `zbujb_admin_powershell "<single-expression effect>"`
662	   or `zbujb_admin_exec LETTER "<single-statement effect>"` runs the
663	   unconditional action only on the bash-side branches that need it.
664	   Each branch uses one zbujb call.
665	
666	Concrete shapes for common cases — file/dir presence, WSL distro
667	membership, local user existence, service state, registry key existence —
668	appear in the "Idempotency Exemplars" section below.
669	
670	### PowerShell boolean serialization
671	
672	`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets serialize
673	as the literal strings `"True"` or `"False"` through
674	`zbujb_powershell_capture` (after WSL_UTF8 normalization and CR stripping).
675	Canonical bash check:
676	
677	```bash
678	if [[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]; then ...
679	```
680	
681	Do not use `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is also non-empty.
682	
683	### Capture failure absorption
684	
685	The capture call's bash exit-status is non-zero on any of: ssh transport
686	failure, PS body trapping its own error under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`,
687	native binary in the body returning non-zero, or wrapper-trailer fire on
688	`$LASTEXITCODE`. Two absorption shapes:
689	
690	```bash
691	# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
692	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
693	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
694	
695	# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
696	z_wsl_list=""
697	z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
698	  || z_wsl_list=""
699	```
700	
701	Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
702	state is a legitimate pre-condition (e.g. WSL not yet installed during
703	first-run purge — the next steps install it).
704	
705	### Open question: bool-returning predicate variant
706	
707	A `zbujb_powershell_predicate` (BCG `_predicate` class — never dies, status
708	only, idiomatic in `if`/`while`) would collapse the capture+compare pair
709	into `if zbujb_powershell_predicate ROLE "Test-Path '...'"; then ...`. The
710	shape was tabled because ssh-layer transport failure adds a third value
711	that doesn't fit either bool answer cleanly: strict BCG predicate semantics
712	("never dies, status only") would conflate transport failure with "false,"
713	reintroducing the OQ-1 silent-swallow this section is meant to close. The
714	canonical CDD shape uses `zbujb_powershell_capture` + `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
715	explicitly so transport failures stay loud. The predicate variant remains
716	an open design question.
717	
718	## Idempotency Exemplars
719	
720	Ready-made CDD shapes for the patterns this codebase actually uses. Copy
721	and adjust the variable names; do not derive from principles each time.
722	Adding a new exemplar earns a paragraph here once verified.
723	
724	### File presence → conditional remove
725	
726	```bash
727	local z_present
728	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
729	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
730	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
731	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
732	    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
733	fi
734	```
735	
736	### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)
737	
738	```bash
739	local z_present
740	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
741	  || buc_die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
742	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
743	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
744	    || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
745	fi
746	```
747	
748	### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister
749	
750	A fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
751	the list capture absorbs failure silently rather than dying.
752	
753	```bash
754	local z_wsl_list=""
755	z_wsl_list=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
756	  || z_wsl_list=""
757	if grep -qFx "${z_dist}" <<<"${z_wsl_list}"; then
758	  zbujb_admin_powershell "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
759	    || buc_die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"
760	fi
761	```
762	
763	### Local user existence → conditional removal
764	
765	`Get-LocalUser`'s `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` is permitted here per
766	PS-8's probe-side carve-out — the cmdlet is non-destructive and "user
767	absent" reads as empty stdout.
768	
769	```bash
770	local z_user_state
771	z_user_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
772	  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
773	  || buc_die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
774	z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
775	if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
776	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
777	    || buc_die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
778	fi
779	```
780	
781	### Service running state → conditional start
782	
783	```bash
784	local z_service_state
785	z_service_state=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged \
786	  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
787	  || buc_die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
788	if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
789	  zbujb_admin_powershell "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
790	    || buc_die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
791	fi
792	```
793	
794	### Registry key absence → conditional creation
795	
796	```bash
797	local z_present
798	z_present=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
799	  || buc_die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
800	if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
801	  zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
802	    || buc_die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"
803	fi
804	```

806	## Wrapper Discipline
807	
808	### PowerShell wrapper (proven shape)
809	
810	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_powershell` after the LASTEXITCODE-init repair:
811	
812	```bash
813	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
814	    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
815	```
816	
817	Discipline:
818	- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
819	- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
820	- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (PS-1)
821	- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
822	- Body uses single quotes for PS literals (PS-3)
823	- Body avoids object-emitting cmdlets immediately before exit, or pipes
824	  them through Out-String first (PS-2)
825	
826	### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (proven shape, w-letter)
827	
828	Mirrors `zbujb_admin_exec w`. Body is a single statement (SH-10),
829	single-line (SH-2), `"` escaped to `\"` (SH-3), and all
830	`$name`/`${name}` references escaped to `\$name`/`\${name}` for
831	deferred bash-side expansion (SH-6). `$(...)` command substitution does
832	not need escape (SH-6).
833	
834	```bash
835	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
836	    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body}\""
837	```
838	
839	Body authoring discipline:
840	- One statement per body (SH-10) — pipelines OK as shape 2, `;`-joins forbidden
841	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
842	- `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}` (SH-6)
843	- `$(...)` left as-is (SH-6)
844	- No heredocs, no `bash -s`, no inner pipe-then-bash (SH-1, SH-5)
845	- No newlines in body (SH-2)
846	- Multi-statement work decomposes into multiple round-trips per the
847	  Capture-Decide-Dispatch pattern, not into a longer body
848	
849	### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (proven shape, c-letter)
850	
851	Cygwin's path is simpler. cmd.exe → C:/cygwin64/bin/bash.exe. No wsl.exe,
852	no Windows-side `$` substitution.
853	
854	```bash
855	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
856	    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body}\""
857	```
858	
859	Body authoring discipline:
860	- One statement per body (SH-10) — pipelines OK as shape 2, `;`-joins forbidden
861	- Embedded `"` → `\"` (SH-3)
862	- `$name`, `${name}`, `$(...)` all unescaped — bash sees them directly (SH-6)
863	- No heredocs, no `bash -s` (SH-1, SH-5)
864	- No newlines in body (SH-2)
865	- Multi-statement work decomposes into multiple round-trips per the
866	  Capture-Decide-Dispatch pattern, not into a longer body
867

912	
913	Every WSG rule has a probe that demonstrates the failure mode and the
914	fix. Standard form:
915	
916	```
917	# Probe N: <claim>
918	# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>
919	
920	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<failing form>"
921	# stdout: <observed>
922	# exit:   <observed>
923	
924	./tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh <node> "<repaired form>"
925	# stdout: <observed>
926	# exit:   <observed>
927	```
928	
929	Probes are read-only and idempotent where possible. Probes that mutate
930	remote state (creating users, files) must declare cleanup or rely on a
931	subsequent obliterate cycle.
932	
933	## Acronym Registry
